retrieved_docs.title: Infraorbital Nerve Block -- Technique or Treatment -- Extraoral Methods
retrieved_docs.content: Anatomic approach: The infraorbital foramen can be located by having the patient look straight ahead while the clinician imagines a line descending from the pupil to the inferior border of the infraorbital ridge, passing through the bicuspid teeth and mental foramen. The clinician should identify the inferior border of the infraorbital rim. The skin over the infraorbital foramen is then cleansed with an antiseptic agent and sterile gauze. The needle is inserted through the skin, subcutaneous tissue, and muscle. Before injecting the anesthetic, the clinician should aspirate to ensure the needle is not within a vessel, then proceed with the injection. Due to the proximity of the facial nerve when using the extraoral approach, it is advisable to select an anesthetic agent without added vasoconstrictors. The overlying tissues should appear edematous after injection, and the area should be massaged for 10 to 15 seconds after removing the needle.

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Gingival Overgrowth -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Plaque control should be the first step in treating DIGO, correct oral hygiene, and professional plaque removal, including tooth surface cleaning and periodic scaling. [20] [21] Control of inflammation, including non-steroidal anti-inflammatory agents, antibiotics to control infection, and topical antifungal medication like nystatin, may also be utilized. Folate supplementation has also been used. [22] A sufficient period of 6 to 12 months should be allowed to elapse after discontinuing the inducing drug before surgery is considered. [19] The surgical methods include gingivectomy and periodontal flap surgery. Electrocautery may be used in difficult cases, children, or where the gingiva is fragile and likely to bleed. The carbon dioxide laser has a wavelength of 10600 nm; hence, it is readily absorbed by water and is effective in the surgery of soft tissues with high water content, like the gingiva. Blood vessels up to a diameter of 0.5 mm can be sealed effectively and provide a dry field for better surgical field visibility. A laser is preferred over the scalpel as it has strong bactericidal and hemostatic effects. [2] [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Open Heart Massage -- Technique or Treatment -- OCM Techniques
retrieved_docs.content: Wise and colleagues recommend a 2-handed technique for OCM, where 1 flat hand is placed beneath the heart's posterior surface and the other on the anterior surface. [9] The heart is then gently compressed from the apex upward at approximately 100 beats per minute (aligning with Advanced Cardiac Life Support guidelines), ensuring not to use fingertips, as they increase the risk of myocardial injury. Alternatively, a single-hand technique can be employed if the clinician's hand is large enough. In this approach, the clinician’s straight fingers are positioned on the heart’s posterior surface near the apex, with the thumb on the anterior surface. Maintaining the heart horizontally is essential during OCM; lifting the apex could hinder venous filling. Additionally, an assistant may compress the descending aorta to maximize blood flow to the coronary arteries and cerebral vasculature. [9] If spontaneous circulation is restored, the incision is covered with sterile, saline-dampened gauze, and antibiotics targeting skin flora are administered. Definitive thoracotomy closure, ideally by a cardiothoracic surgeon, is recommended as soon as possible.

retrieved_docs.title: Open Heart Massage -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Coordination with ancillary services Immediate communication with the blood bank ensures prompt availability of blood products for transfusion, as many patients with OCM have extensive blood loss. Lab support is also on standby to expedite critical labs, such as blood gases and coagulation studies.

retrieved_docs.title: Supertrochlear Nerve Block -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Lastly, the needle should be removed and pressure applied to avoid bleeding complications. Providers can also gently massage the area to aid in hemostasis and dispersing the anesthetic, respectively.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Teeth -- Technique or Treatment -- Achieving Hemostasis
retrieved_docs.content: Following the removal of a tooth, a roll of gauze or bite pack should be placed over the socket, and the patient should be asked to bite together or hold down on the pack firmly. This pressure should be placed for a minimum of five minutes, and hemostasis should be checked before the patient can leave the chair. If hemostasis has not been achieved after a prolonged period of pressure, then local hemostatic agents should be used. The above steps outline the usual sequence of simple removal of a tooth. Should these steps not be successful in removing a tooth, then a surgical approach may be required.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteopathic Manipulative Treatment: Lymphatic Procedures -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: The practitioner should also be mindful when doing lymphatic treatments during certain infections. For example, there is a hypothetical risk that lymphatic treatments may further disseminate diseases in the case of bacteremia or an abscess about to burst.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral and Maxillofacial Surgery, Facial Laceration Repair -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: After obtaining adequate anesthesia and before any suturing, the wound must be thoroughly irrigated to remove debris and blood. Cleaning the wound decreases the risk of infection and improves visualization of tissue layers and surface landmarks. Warm sterile saline irrigation alone is adequate to cleanse facial lacerations. Antiseptic solutions such as dilute povidone-iodine and sodium hypochlorite have bactericidal properties but can inhibit fibroblast cell growth and delay overall wound healing. [45] [46] Thorough irrigation is also important to prevent traumatic tattooing. Leaving pigmented debris such as gravel or shrapnel in a wound bed covered by epithelium can cause traumatic tattoos with continued wound manipulation. [47] After irrigation and debris removal, the wound bed must be inspected for hemostasis. Active bleeding may require direct pressure or vessel ligation.

retrieved_docs.content: Airway management The patient’s airway is secured (typically by intubation) to maintain adequate oxygenation during OCM. An anesthesia clinician or other skilled clinician manages ventilation, usually with 100% oxygen, to optimize tissue perfusion.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2651
retrieved_docs.content: Varicose vein ligation and stripping directly targets the vein (vény), reflecting that this surgical procedure is performed on the vascular structures themselves. The vein (vény) also has an indirect procedural association with blood flow (إراقة الدم), underscoring its essential role in circulating blood throughout the body. This relationship highlights how the treatment of varicose veins is intrinsically linked to both the anatomical site of the veins and their functional involvement in blood movement.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2034
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin K and other hemostatics produce hemostatické účinky, meaning they exert the physiological hemostatic effects that stop bleeding by promoting clot formation and maintaining vascular integrity. These hemostatic actions are central to the therapeutic role of vitamin K and related agents in controlling hemorrhage and supporting normal coagulation processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2380

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2158
retrieved_docs.content: Varicose vein ligation and stripping is a surgical technique that is performed directly on veins, specifically targeting the abnormal, dilated vessels known as varicose veins. The procedure involves ligating (tying off) the affected vein to prevent blood flow and then stripping (removing) the vein segment, thereby alleviating symptoms such as pain, swelling, and cosmetic concerns. By acting directly on the vein, this intervention addresses the underlying venous insufficiency that leads to varicosities and contributes to improved lower‑extremity circulation and patient quality of life.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1131
retrieved_docs.content: Procedure on artery is classified as a type of systemic arterial pressure monitoring, indicating that interventions involving arterial access are employed to measure and assess systemic arterial blood pressure in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1807
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose encompass the specific intervention of control of epistaxis, which is classified as a type of nasal procedure. The control of epistaxis is performed directly on the nose (nos), indicating that the nose is the precise anatomical site targeted by this bleeding‑control technique.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1232
retrieved_docs.content: Operative procedure on vein is a surgical intervention that is performed directly on the vén (vein), establishing the vein as the primary anatomical site of the operation. In addition, the vein serves as an indirect procedural site for إراقة الدم (blood‑drainage) procedures, indicating that while blood‑drainage interventions may not act directly on the vein, the vessel is nonetheless involved in the pathway of such treatments. This dual role of the vein—as a direct target for operative procedures and an indirect conduit for blood‑drainage techniques—underscores its central importance in vascular interventions and related patient care strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1053
retrieved_docs.content: Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium is a broad diagnostic category that includes COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY. Within this category, a specific manifestation is Spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester, which is further classified as Early, first or unspecified trimester hemorrhage. Recognizing that spotting in an unspecified trimester represents an early‑trimester hemorrhagic event and is considered a pregnancy‑related complication helps clinicians organize maternal conditions that affect pregnancy, delivery, or the puerperium and guides appropriate assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2676
retrieved_docs.content: Stripping of lower limb varicose veins is classified as a vascular surgical procedure on the lower limb, representing a specific operative technique used to treat varicose veins by removing or ligating the diseased superficial veins. This procedure falls within the broader category of lower‑limb vascular surgeries, which encompass various interventions aimed at restoring normal venous and arterial function in the legs.
